skill.name: scribe-mcp-usage
skill.description: Operate Scribe MCP workflows for project registration, documentation updates, progress logs, and read-only project diagnostics.
skill.summary: Guide agents through Scribe MCP project setup, documentation lifecycle tasks, and auditable progress logging.
skill.author: CortaLabs

security_audit.summary: Static command, filesystem, and environment hits are mostly false positives caused by Markdown examples, placeholder paths, and variable names. The substantive risk is prompt-style authority language that tells agents to treat the skill as non-negotiable policy and to log broad work details. No evidence found of bundled executable code, network exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: The skill uses non-negotiable commandment language, claims broad MCP tool access, and requires agents to log every action. This can override platform policy, change task workflow, and cause sensitive project details to be written into logs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly directs agent behavior with absolute language and broad logging requirements. I did not find exfiltration or malware, so confidence is high for prompt-control risk but not critical malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Audit Logging Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to log investigations, decisions, code changes, test results, and missed steps. This is useful for audit trails but can capture private user context if applied without filtering.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logging scope is directly stated and repeated in the skill. The risk depends on runtime data handled by users, so it is moderate rather than confirmed data exposure.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Command Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown examples and inline tool signatures, not executable Ruby or shell code shipped by the skill. They should still be reviewed before users copy commands into a terminal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside fenced or inline Markdown documentation. No executable source file or automatic command invocation was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder Path Examples
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are placeholder paths in documented responses, such as abbreviated absolute paths. No file read or write code uses these paths in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is response examples in Markdown and uses ellipses as placeholders. I found no code that resolves user-controlled traversal paths.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment Variable Mention
low_findings.description: The environment access finding is a documented paid-test gate naming an API key variable. The skill does not read the variable or transmit secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable appears as explanatory text for skipping paid tests by default. No executable access to environment values was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Policy Override Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill presents workflow rules as absolute mandates and describes consequences for violations. Marketplace users could mistake these instructions for higher-priority system policy.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in headings and repeated imperative text. The issue is semantic prompt control, not conventional code execution.

content.value_statement: Teams lose context when project documentation and progress logs drift from daily work. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Scribe MCP setup, document updates, and auditable logging.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to call set_project for a repository.
Describes manage_docs actions for architecture, phase plan, and checklist files.
Shows append_entry patterns for single and bulk progress logging.
Documents read_recent and query_entries for scoped log review.
Lists Scribe MCP tool signatures and expected payload fields.
Provides guidance for rotating logs and generating document templates.
limitations: Requires a configured local Scribe MCP server.
Does not include executable scripts or server implementation files.
Uses strong agent instructions that need marketplace moderation.
Examples assume the repository follows the Scribe documentation layout.

use_cases.title: Start a tracked implementation project
use_cases.description: A developer can initialize Scribe project context and prepare architecture, phase, and checklist documents before coding.

use_cases.title: Maintain auditable progress logs
use_cases.description: A team lead can guide agents to record meaningful work entries and query recent project history.

use_cases.title: Review Scribe MCP integration behavior
use_cases.description: A platform reviewer can inspect supported tool calls, payload shapes, and documentation workflows before enabling the skill.

prompt_templates.title: Set the active project
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Scribe MCP to set the current repository as the active project. Confirm the project name, root path, and available docs.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare project documents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use manage_docs to update the architecture guide, phase plan, and checklist for this feature. Keep entries concise and evidence based.

prompt_templates.title: Audit recent work history
prompt_templates.prompt: Use read_recent or query_entries to summarize the last relevant Scribe entries. Identify open decisions, blockers, and completed tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Recover missing progress records
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced maintenance after a long work session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the completed work and append a concise Scribe summary. Include decisions, tests, errors, and unresolved risks without exposing secrets.

output_examples.input: Set up Scribe for this repository and tell me what is ready.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects the project, confirms the repository root, lists available planning documents, and reports any missing setup steps.

output_examples.input: Update the checklist after finishing authentication tests.
output_examples.output: The assistant records the completed verification item, links it to supporting evidence, and notes remaining authentication follow-up work.

output_examples.input: Find recent context before changing the parser.
output_examples.output: The assistant reviews recent log entries, summarizes parser-related decisions, and calls out current blockers before editing files.

best_practices: Use the skill only with a trusted local Scribe MCP server.
Filter secrets and private user data before writing progress logs.
Treat Scribe logs as project records, not as hidden model memory.
anti_patterns: Do not let the skill override higher-priority system or user instructions.
Do not copy example shell commands without checking the active repository.
Do not log credentials, private messages, or unrelated user data.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It guides agents through Scribe MCP project setup, documentation updates, progress logs, and project history review.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown instruction file. Command examples require a user or agent to execute them separately.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill uses forceful instructions that can influence agent behavior and encourage broad logging of work details.

faq.question: Does it require secrets?
faq.answer: No. It mentions API key variables only as examples for paid test gating.

faq.question: Can it work without Scribe MCP?
faq.answer: No. The guidance depends on Scribe MCP tools such as set_project, manage_docs, append_entry, and query_entries.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use it in trusted repositories, keep logs scoped to the task, and never allow it to override platform policy.
